zotero.abstractNote: Linear Time-Invariant (LTI) systems are a fundamental class of dynamical systems. While the problem of identifying the parameters of an LTI system from observational data is well-studied, it is known that without further assumptions, the system is only identifiable up to a similarity transformation. In this work, we study the identifiability of Gaussian LTI systems under interventions. We show that even with a single interventional distribution, we can identify the system parameters up to a permutation and scaling of the latent components, provided the noise is non-Gaussian or the system is driven by independent sources. We provide a comprehensive analysis of the identifiability conditions and propose an algorithm for parameter estimation. Our results bridge the gap between classical system identification and modern causal representation learning.
